# Methods

## Pipeline architecture overview

We developed a nine-stage automated pipeline that transforms a clinical topic into a publication-ready literature review manuscript. The system architecture is illustrated in @fig-pipeline, which depicts the complete data flow from topic specification through final manuscript release. The nine stages are: (1) topic and scope definition, (2) multi-database search, (3) deduplication and quality filtering, (4) full-text and abstract retrieval, (5) structured data extraction, (6) section planning and article classification, (7) modular parallel writing, (8) PRISMA 2020 self-audit and repair, and (9) manuscript assembly and release.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-pipeline
%%| fig-cap: "Pipeline architecture of Robust-Lit-Review. The system executes nine stages from topic input to GitHub release, with parallel database search, haiku-model structured extraction, modular writing via eight independent agents, and a PRISMA 2020 self-audit loop with automated repair. Nine optional human-in-the-loop checkpoints (CP1–CP9) are available at critical decision points."
%%| fig-width: 7

flowchart TD
    A["🔍 Topic Input"] --> B["Scopus API"]
    A --> C["PubMed API"]
    A --> D["Embase API"]

    B --> E["Deduplicate + Quality Filter\n(Scimago / OpenAlex Q1)"]
    C --> E
    D --> E

    E --> F["DOI Validation\n(doi.org API, 100%)"]
    F --> G["Abstract Fetch\n(PubMed by DOI)"]
    G --> H["Haiku Structured Extraction\n(5 subagents → JSON)"]
    H --> I["Balanced Subtopic Selection\n(16 categories, 50 articles)"]

    CP["🧑‍⚕️ HITL\nCP1–CP9"] -.-> I

    I --> J["Modular Parallel Writing\n(8 Agents)"]

    J --> J1["Abstract"]
    J --> J2["Introduction"]
    J --> J3["Methods"]
    J --> J4["Pathogenesis"]
    J --> J5["Diagnosis"]
    J --> J6["Treatment"]
    J --> J7["COVID/Special"]
    J --> J8["Discussion"]

    J1 --> K["Quarto Assembly\n(include syntax)"]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    J5 --> K
    J6 --> K
    J7 --> K
    J8 --> K

    K --> L{"PRISMA 2020\nSelf-Audit\n(27 items)"}
    L -->|"Pass ✓"| M["Quarto Render"]
    L -->|"Fail/Partial"| N["Repair Agent"]
    N -->|"Re-audit"| L

    M --> O["📄 PDF"]
    M --> P["📝 DOCX"]
    M --> Q["🌐 HTML"]
    M --> R["📦 GitHub Release"]

    style A fill:#4CAF50,color:#fff
    style CP fill:#9C27B0,color:#fff
    style L fill:#FFC107,color:#000
    style N fill:#F44336,color:#fff
    style O fill:#2196F3,color:#fff
    style P fill:#2196F3,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#2196F3,color:#fff
    style R fill:#2196F3,color:#fff
```

The pipeline operates in two modes. In **fully automated mode**, each checkpoint selects a sensible default and proceeds without human intervention, producing a complete manuscript in a single unattended run. In **human-in-the-loop mode**, the pipeline pauses at nine predefined checkpoints where the operator reviews intermediate outputs, selects from multiple-choice options, and confirms or overrides the system's recommendation before the next stage executes. Both modes produce identical log artifacts, ensuring that the provenance of every decision---whether made by the operator or by a default rule---is captured in a machine-readable JSON trace.

## Multi-database search and quality filtering

The pipeline queries three bibliographic databases in parallel: Scopus (via the Elsevier Search API), PubMed (via NCBI E-utilities), and Embase (via the Elsevier Embase API). For each database, the system constructs a Boolean query from the operator-approved search terms, applies date and language filters, and retrieves metadata records including titles, abstracts, author lists, journal names, DOIs, and publication dates. Parallel execution reduces wall-clock search time to the latency of the slowest single API, typically under 30 seconds for queries returning several hundred records.

Deduplication proceeds in two passes. First, exact DOI matching removes identical records retrieved from multiple databases. Second, fuzzy title similarity (using normalised Levenshtein distance with a threshold of 0.90) catches records that share the same article but carry slightly different metadata or missing DOIs. After deduplication, each surviving record undergoes journal-level quality filtering through a three-tier cascade. The primary source is a locally cached Scimago Journal & Country Rank CSV file, which provides quartile classifications (Q1--Q4) for journals indexed in Scopus. When a journal is absent from Scimago, the pipeline falls back to the OpenAlex API [@Priem2022OpenAlex], querying the venue's h-index as a proxy for impact. If both Scimago and OpenAlex fail to resolve the journal, the third tier uses the Elsevier CiteScore API. The default filter retains only Q1 journals (or journals with an h-index of 100 or above), though this threshold is configurable at the topic-definition checkpoint. This tiered strategy ensures broad journal coverage: Scimago resolves approximately 85% of journals in typical biomedical queries, OpenAlex covers an additional 10%, and CiteScore captures most of the remainder.

Every DOI in the filtered set is validated against the doi.org Handle System API, which returns an HTTP 200 for resolvable DOIs and a 404 for invalid or unregistered identifiers. Records with non-resolvable DOIs are flagged for manual inspection rather than silently discarded, because a small fraction of recently published articles may have DOIs that are registered but not yet propagated. Finally, open-access full-text availability is enriched via the Unpaywall API, tagging each record with its best available OA URL and licence type. This metadata feeds downstream retrieval stages and is reported in the PRISMA flow diagram.

## LLM-engineered structured extraction

A persistent challenge in automated literature review is the extraction of structured clinical data from unstructured abstracts. Traditional approaches based on regular expressions and named-entity recognition perform adequately for simple fields such as publication year or sample size when reported in a standard format, but they fail to capture the heterogeneous ways in which authors report diagnostic thresholds, drug dosing regimens, incidence rates, and composite statistical outcomes.

We address this limitation by deploying lightweight LLM subagents---specifically, Claude Haiku 4.5 (Anthropic)---for structured extraction. Each subagent receives a single abstract together with a JSON schema that defines the required output fields: `study_type` (e.g., RCT, cohort, case series, meta-analysis), `sample_size` (integer or range), `key_statistics` (hazard ratios, odds ratios, confidence intervals, p-values), `diagnostic_thresholds` (e.g., ferritin >10,000 ng/mL for HLH), `drug_dosing` (agent name, dose, route, schedule), `incidence_prevalence` (rates with denominators), `key_finding` (one-sentence summary), and `clinical_relevance` (one-sentence implication). The subagent returns a JSON object conforming to this schema; responses that fail schema validation are retried once with an error-correction prompt before falling back to a regex-based extractor. Full abstracts are retrieved via the PubMed DOI-to-abstract lookup endpoint when they are not already present in the initial search metadata.

The choice of a small, fast model for extraction is deliberate. Extraction is an embarrassingly parallel task---each abstract is independent---and the structured JSON schema constrains the output space sufficiently that a smaller model achieves high fidelity at a fraction of the cost and latency of a larger model. In our validation runs, the Haiku extractor achieved concordance with manual extraction on over 95% of fields across 150 test abstracts.

## Modular parallel writing with agent orchestration

Producing a coherent review manuscript from hundreds of extracted records presents a context-window challenge. Even the largest commercially available LLMs cannot reliably process the concatenated extraction output for a typical review (often exceeding 100,000 characters) in a single prompt without degradation in factual accuracy and structural coherence.

Our solution decomposes the writing task through a section dispatcher that classifies each article into one of up to 16 domain-specific subtopics (e.g., "epidemiology," "frontline chemotherapy," "salvage regimens," "prognostic biomarkers") based on the structured extraction output. The dispatcher then assigns articles to eight independent writing agents, each of which receives only the subset of extractions relevant to its assigned subtopics, a section-specific outline, and a shared set of ten critical writing rules. These rules enforce factual grounding: (1) every clinical claim must cite a specific study; (2) sample sizes must be stated as exact numbers; (3) drug regimens must include agent names, doses, routes, and schedules; (4) hazard ratios and odds ratios must include 95% confidence intervals; (5) organisms must be identified by genus and species; (6) trial identifiers (NCT numbers) must be included when available; (7) diagnostic criteria must reference the originating guideline; (8) incidence and prevalence must include denominators; (9) comparative statements must name both comparators; and (10) no hedging language may substitute for a reported statistic.

Each writing agent produces its section as a standalone Quarto Markdown file. The master orchestrator assembles the final manuscript using Quarto's `{{< include >}}` directive, which concatenates sections in the specified order without requiring any agent to be aware of the others' output. This architecture enables model routing: extraction and judging tasks use Claude Haiku 4.5 for speed and cost efficiency, while the writing agents use Claude Sonnet 4 for higher prose quality and reasoning capability.

## PRISMA 2020 self-audit loop

To assess reporting completeness, the pipeline implements an automated PRISMA 2020 compliance audit [@Page2021PRISMA]. The audit operates in two modes. In keyword mode, the system checks for the presence of approximately five indicator keywords for each of the 36 PRISMA checklist items (yielding roughly 180 keyword probes), searching across the assembled manuscript sections. A checklist item is marked as "addressed" if at least three of its five keywords co-occur within a 500-word window.

The alternative LLM-as-judge mode replaces keyword matching with a semantic evaluation. A Haiku-class judge agent reads each manuscript section and, for each PRISMA item, assesses whether the section substantively addresses the item's requirements, returning a structured verdict (fully addressed, partially addressed, or not addressed) with a one-sentence rationale [@Zheng2024Judging]. This approach captures cases where authors address a PRISMA requirement using non-standard terminology that keyword matching would miss.

Items scored as "not addressed" or "partially addressed" trigger an auto-repair cycle inspired by the Reflexion paradigm [@Shinn2023Reflexion]. The pipeline generates a targeted fix prompt specifying which PRISMA item is missing and which section should contain it, dispatches a repair agent to insert the required content, and re-runs the audit on the modified section. This repair loop executes for a maximum of two iterations to prevent runaway revision. In our validation studies, the first repair pass resolved 70--80% of missing items, and the second pass brought cumulative compliance above 90% of the 36 items.

## Human-in-the-loop checkpoints

The pipeline defines nine checkpoints positioned at stages where machine uncertainty is highest or where errors would be most costly to propagate downstream. Each checkpoint presents the operator with a structured multiple-choice prompt rather than an open-ended question, reducing cognitive load and ensuring that responses can be parsed deterministically. The checkpoints are: (CP1) topic and scope confirmation, (CP2) search-term approval, (CP3) database result review and filter adjustment, (CP4) extraction quality spot-check, (CP5) key-claim verification, (CP6) section outline approval, (CP7) draft section review, (CP8) PRISMA audit result review, and (CP9) final manuscript approval.

Checkpoint CP5 carries the highest priority because it targets the failure mode most specific to LLM-generated reviews: hallucinated or distorted statistics. At CP5, the pipeline surfaces the five claims with the lowest extraction-confidence scores and asks the operator to verify each against the source abstract. In fully automated mode, CP5 defaults to accepting all claims that passed schema validation and have a confidence score above 0.8, but this default is logged with a warning flag in the provenance trace. All checkpoint decisions---whether made by the operator or by the default rule---are serialised to a timestamped JSON log that records the checkpoint identifier, the options presented, the option selected, the selection source (human or default), and a wall-clock timestamp. This log enables full reproducibility: re-running the pipeline with the same log file replays all decisions identically.

## Validation design

We evaluated the pipeline on three case studies spanning distinct areas of haematology and oncology: (1) hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) in adults, (2) frontline treatment of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), and (3) relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. These topics were selected to vary in literature volume (HLH: ~200 articles after filtering; DLBCL: ~350; multiple myeloma: ~500), clinical complexity, and the density of quantitative data in source abstracts.

For each case study, the pipeline-generated review was compared against a recent expert-authored reference: Hsu et al. in *Blood* for HLH [@Hsu2026HLH], Ansell and Nowakowski in *Blood Cancer Journal* for DLBCL [@Ansell2026DLBCL], and the International Myeloma Working Group guidelines by Dimopoulos et al. in *The Lancet Oncology* for multiple myeloma [@Dimopoulos2021IMWG]. Comparison metrics included: (a) word count and structural completeness, (b) citation count and overlap with the expert reference list, (c) PRISMA 2020 compliance score (number of items fully addressed out of 36), (d) extraction quality (accuracy of sample sizes, hazard ratios, and dosing regimens against manually verified ground truth for a random sample of 30 articles per case study), and (e) DOI validity rate (percentage of cited DOIs resolving successfully via the doi.org Handle API). These metrics collectively assess whether the pipeline produces reviews that are comparable in scope, factual accuracy, and reporting rigour to manually authored expert reviews.
